Case Study: Healthcare Platform

Transforming a free healthcare job board into a scalable subscription platform

NPHIRE had validated demand through a free product. The next step was monetization. But before defining any strategy, I needed to understand what paying customers would actually need.

Images were adapted to respect NDA and compliance regulations.

User Journeys User Stories Acceptance Criteria GTM Strategy AI Feature Design API Integrations Roadmap Ownership

The challenge

The task was to define the paid product from scratch: what tiers to offer, what integrations were needed and where there was friction in the existing free experience that could stop conversion.

Understanding the user

I started by reviewing the existing free employer journeys, mapping the flows and validating them with our data analytics, market research and client interviews. That information was condensed and documented through artifacts like User Story Maps and Impact Maps.

Three key findings came out of this work that drove product decisions:

Finding 01
Employers had very different hiring needs. One tier wouldn't cover them
Some healthcare companies and clinics hired one NP occasionally, others needed to manage high-volume pipelines. To cover all cases, I proposed a 3-plan model with add-ons to offer different levels of analytics, metrics, amount of job postings, amount of seats per team account, etc.
Finding 02
Recruiters already had their ATS and we had to fit into it
Recruiters weren't going to adopt a new platform from scratch just for NP hires. Integration with their existing tools, the main one being Greenhouse, became an MVP requirement.
Finding 03
The main pain point in applications was quality, not quantity
The biggest frustration I heard in interviews wasn't volume but rather incomplete profiles or vague descriptions. To cover this need, we created an AI-powered resume parsing feature for applicants, so they didn't have to manually fill out every application.

Results and Impacts

Research findings were synthesized into user needs, documented in a PRD as the Why and the What of the product and translated into technical specifications (functional and non-functional requirements, user stories, acceptance criteria, API integration requirements). Those items were later prioritized using the MoSCoW and Eisenhower matrix to define the MVP: quick wins and big projects were classified as Must items, while low-hanging fruits were positioned at the top of the backlog to include only if there was extra time.
Lastly, KPIs were selected to measure success based on the user needs identified: amount of new clients, application time (for applicants) and % of incomplete profiles. The final solution included three defined pricing tiers, integrations (Stripe, Greenhouse) and an AI-powered resume parsing feature using OpenAI designed to reduce application friction. We obtained 15 new employers within the first 2 weeks, which exceeded expectations and secured the next round of investments.

−15%
Incomplete profiles
20→5 min
Application time
15
New customers in first 2 weeks

Case Study: Enterprise AI Platform

Making AI feel safe for highly regulated companies

Halo is a sovereign AI platform that gives enterprise teams governed access to leading AI models without sending proprietary data to external APIs. Merlin is the first app built on it: a natural-language interface that synthesizes internal data into cited answers in under 30 seconds.

Images were adapted to respect NDA and compliance regulations.

Product Strategy Competitive Research Information Architecture Guardrail Requirements UX Flows Roadmap Definition

The challenge

EVOCs clients were highly regulated large enterprises managing sensitive proprietary data: market intelligence, internal reports, operational records. Their employees were watching the rest of the world get faster and smarter with AI tools and wanted the same, but using ChatGPT or Claude directly meant sending confidential data to an external API, which was a compliance violation.

Understanding the user

There were two competing stakeholders with conflicting needs. Employees wanted AI productivity: faster research, faster answers, less manual work. IT and compliance teams needed to maintain data sovereignty, audit trails and policy enforcement. A blanket ban satisfied compliance but frustrated employees and widened the gap with competitors who operated in less restricted environments.

The key insight was that the problem wasn't about which AI model to use. It was about where the data went. If the platform kept data inside the client's own controlled environment while still providing access to leading models, both sides could win. That became the foundation of Halo's design.

Three design decisions shaped the platform:

Design decision 01
Deployed inside the client's Azure environment, not hosted externally
The platform runs on the client's own infrastructure. No data leaves their environment. No vendor has access to their prompts or documents. This was the prerequisite that made everything else possible: employees could use AI without creating a compliance exposure.
Design decision 02
Model-agnostic by design, governed at the gateway
Employees can choose their preferred model (Claude, GPT or others) across thousands of frontier, open and fine-tuned options. What they don't control is what data goes in. Policy guardrails are enforced at the gateway layer before any prompt reaches a model, with a 7-year tamper-resistant audit trail on every interaction.
Design decision 03
Same question, different answers by design
A general employee and a compliance admin asking the same question should see different responses, scoped to their permission level. Access is managed via existing company SSO credentials, with no new accounts and no new passwords. Each team gets a personalized workspace showing only the tools relevant to their role.

Results and Impacts

Halo was designed from the start as a modular platform: a governed base layer that any internal AI tool can be built on top of. New apps inherit the same access control, SSO and audit infrastructure automatically, without each team having to rebuild compliance from scratch. The product narrative I developed for client conversations was: Halo isn't a tool. It's the infrastructure that makes every future AI tool in your organization safe to build.

Merlin was the first app built on top of Halo. It gives enterprise employees a natural-language interface to query proprietary internal data and returns sourced, cited answers in under 30 seconds. Every claim is traced back to its source document, with version and section, making answers defensible in audits and eliminating the reliance on informal channels that had previously undermined executive confidence.

<30s
Time to cited answer in Merlin
−50%
Time spent on market research and marketing tasks
7yr
Tamper-resistant audit trail

Case Study: Identity & Access Governance

Creating the access governance tool with the fastest setup

Enterprise access reviews are mandatory for regulatory compliance, but existing Identity Governance tools required a minimun of 9 months and significant budget just to get started. AURA was built to close that gap: a production-ready access governance platform that connects to a company's corporate directory in minutes and makes access reviews auditable, traceable and fast.

Due to NDA restrictions, product visuals have been simplified. Screen designs and wireframes produced during this project cannot be shown publicly.

Identity Governance Product Discovery Compliance Enterprise SaaS On-premise Solution Design Roadmap Ownership

The challenge

Access reviews are mandatory for regulatory compliance and require complete traceability of every decision. Done manually, they take a significant amount of hours, but tools available in the market took between 9 months and a year to be implemented. The challenge was to provide an access governance tool that simplified the audit process with a faster setup than its competitors, while still allowing complex access control categorization.

Understanding the user

I led solution design end-to-end. At the stage of Discovery, I conducted interviews with security specialists, analysed existing review workflows, ran competitive research across the IGA market and did a deep read of compliance documentation to understand what auditability actually required.

Three findings shaped the product direction:

Finding 01
Implementation complexity was a major adoption barrier
Existing tools required months of configuration before organizations could run a single access review. The setup cost alone was deterring mid-market adoption.
Finding 02
Compliance requirements demanded full auditability across every action
Every approval, rejection and escalation needed to be traceable and exportable. Partial audit trails were a compliance risk, not a minor inconvenience.
Finding 03
Security Administrators and Reviewers had distinct goals and required different experiences
I defined those 2 user personas and created user journeys for each one. Admins needed control and visibility over campaigns; Reviewers needed clarity and speed.

From research to product

The solution included role-based access controls, campaign management with configurable approval logic, a full audit trail of every access decision and a compliance report generator. It connected directly to Microsoft Entra ID to pull the corporate directory and supported both multi-tenant SaaS and on-premise deployment.

All discovery insights were organized into a structured spreadsheet that mapped the full Why, What and How of the product. The Why was built from impact mapping sessions, competitive research findings and discovery insights gathered from interviews. The What and How translated those needs into user stories, acceptance criteria and functional and non-functional requirements, with each item traceable back to its originating insight.

At the same time, EVOCs leadership wanted to explore how AI could be introduced into our product development workflows. To do that in a structured and responsible way, I defined a 3-PRD framework to guide the adoption:

PRD 01
PoC: controlled AI experimentation
Minimal version of requirements to allow developers to create fast prototypes with AI tools and demo them internally. We used this approach to quickly gather feedback without spending too much time on development.
PRD 02
Innovation: integrating validated use cases into the workflow
Once the PoC phase facilitated design decisions, I created a second PRD incorporating findings from my user and market research and the insights gained during the PoC. Still small and without aspects needed for production (like implementation and deployment details), but detailed enough for the dev team to build a solid MVP with an AI tool.
PRD 03
Delivery: rollout and governance
The final PRD was ready for developers to build a production-ready solution assisted by AI tools. It incorporated implementation and deployment specifications as well as a UAT specifically designed for AI-assisted development.

Results and Impacts

Discovery surfaced two client profiles with fundamentally incompatible requirements. The first group needed speed: they wanted to start running access reviews immediately, without a months-long IT project. For them, a cloud-hosted multi-tenant model worked: just two credentials (client ID and client secret) and EVOCs pulls the entire corporate directory from Entra ID, creating a dedicated tenant and making data browsable in minutes. The second group couldn't send their access data to an external environment at all: data sovereignty and internal compliance policies required everything to stay inside their own network. They were willing to accept a longer implementation in exchange for full control.

Those two incompatible needs defined the roadmap. The cloud-hosted version became the MVP: fast to ship, fast to sell and usable as a working demo environment. The on-premise version was scoped in parallel, with a broader feature set including support for identity providers beyond Entra ID.

Our first 3 clients, massive data centers, reported a reduction of 60% in access review activities.

60%
Reduction in access review cycle time
10,000+
Access records managed
